coredata.dc:title: Selective fluorescent probe for sensitive determination of bisphenol A based on molecularly imprinted polymers decorated carbon dots derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Zhang, Yue
Niu, Jingwen
Nie, Linchun
Huang, Shiyu
Liu, Huanhuan
Yuan, Shuai
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Bisphenol A (BPA) is an endocrine disrupting chemical and poses a grave threat to the human health. Herein, a fluorescent probe constructed with molecularly imprinted polymers decorated carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) was proposed for determination of BPA with high selectivity. The CDs@MIPs were constructed using BPA, 4-vinylpyridine and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as template, functional monomer and cross linker, respectively. The obtained fluorescent probe not only owned a highly selective recognition function derived from MIPs but also displayed an excellent sensitivity for sensing BPA stemmed from CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs was varied before and after the removal of BPA templates. The fluorescent decrease fraction of the fluorescent probe demonstrates a nice linearity in BPA concentration range of 10–2000 nM (r2 = 0.9998) and the detection limit is as low as 1.5 nM. The fluorescent probe was triumphantly utilized to sense the level of BPA in real aqueous and plastic samples with good results. Moreover, the fluorescent probe offered a wonderful means for fast identification and sensitive detection of BPA from environmental aqueous samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent probe
Bisphenol A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of BPA in real samples
Optimization for BPA detection based on CDs@MIPs
Reproductivity and stability for determination of BPA
Detection of BPA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the structure of the fluorescent probe and the working principle for the detection of BPA.

(A) TEM image of the CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of the CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption (a) and FL emission (b) spectra of the CDs. (D) FL spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (E) Effect of the pH values on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (F) Effect of the concentration of NaCl solution on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.

(A) TEM image of the CDs@MIPs; (B) UV–vis absorption (a) and fluorescence emission (b) spectra of the CDs@MIPs; (C) FTIR spectra of CDs@MIPs before (1) and after (2) the removal of the template molecules and CDs@NIPs (3).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@NIPs (1), CDs@MIPs before (2) and after (3) the removal of the template molecules, CDs@MIPs with the addition of 1 μM BPA solution (4); (B) The optimization of CDs@MIPs concentration; (C) The optimization of the pH value; (D) The optimization of the reaction time.

(A) Selectivity of CDs@MIPs for BPA over other phenolic compounds (blue bars) and the mixture of BPA with other phenolic compounds (red bars). (B) Selectivity of CDs@NIPs for BPA over other phenolic compounds (blue bars) and the mixture of BPA with other phenolic compounds (magenta bars).
Comparison of different probes for the detection of BPA.
Results of the detection of BPA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Selective fluorescent probe for sensitive determination of bisphenol A based on molecularly imprinted polymers decorated carbon dots derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control
Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is an endocrine disrupting chemical and poses a grave threat to the human health. Herein, a fluorescent probe constructed with molecularly imprinted polymers decorated carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) was proposed for determination of BPA with high selectivity. The CDs@MIPs were constructed using BPA, 4-vinylpyridine and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as template, functional monomer and cross linker, respectively. The obtained fluorescent probe not only owned a highly selective recognition function derived from MIPs but also displayed an excellent sensitivity for sensing BPA stemmed from CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs was varied before and after the removal of BPA templates. The fluorescent decrease fraction of the fluorescent probe demonstrates a nice linearity in BPA concentration range of 10–2000 nM (r2 = 0.9998) and the detection limit is as low as 1.5 nM. The fluorescent probe was triumphantly utilized to sense the level of BPA in real aqueous and plastic samples with good results. Moreover, the fluorescent probe offered a wonderful means for fast identification and sensitive detection of BPA from environmental aqueous samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is mainly employed to synthesize polymer materials such as polycarbonates and epoxy resins, which are widely used in plastic products (drinking-cups, feeding-bottles, etc), food and beverage can inner coatings (Zeng et al., 2021). What's more, it is also a kind of environmental endocrine disruptor (Lei et al., 2021). The BPA presented in the feeding-bottles can induce precocious puberty and present a significant risk to the health of fetus and children (Almeida et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020). Meanwhile, it also causes diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, impaired reproductive capacity and even has carcinogenicity (Völkel et al., 2008; Migeot et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2014). In view of this, it is of great significance to establish new highly sensitive and selective methods for BPA. Until now, multiple methods have been used for sensitive determination of BPA such as chromatography (Watabe et al., 2004; Filippou et al., 2017), chromatography-mass spectrometry (Shen et al., 2005; Zimmers et al., 2014), electrochemical detection (Gao et al., 2012; Deiminiat et al., 2017), chemiluminescence detection (Wang et al., 2005; Ragavan and Rastogi, 2016) and fluorescence detection (Zhu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016). Among these methods, fluorescence detection has become a common method due to its unique advantages such as simplicity, reliability and low cost. Meanwhile, BPA is present in complex matrix medium, and exists in environment samples at trace residue, it is an important issue to developed novel detection methods for BPA with high selectivity in environmental samples.

The molecular imprinting technology is popular to achieve selective recognition and strong binding ability for the specific target molecules (called template molecules) by designable polymers (Chen et al., 2011), and the polymers prepared by this technique are named molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). MIPs have “excellent memory function” for target template molecules, and further own specific recognition for them because there are a considerable number of imprinted holes which highly match the size, shape and functional group of template molecules (Yang et al., 2018a). Besides the high selectivity for template molecules, MIPs have the advantages of simple preparation, low cost and high stability, which have played vital roles in sensor, solid phase extraction, artificial antibody and other fields (Kunath et al., 2015; Sarafraz-Yazdi and Razavi, 2015; Tiwari and Prasad, 2015; Wackerlig and Lieberzeit, 2015; Yang et al., 2016; Panagiotopoulou et al., 2017; Boulanouar et al., 2018).

Fluorescence detection can combine with molecularly imprinted technique through the synthesis of fluorescent MIPs which are obtained by using fluorescent monomers, fluorescent template molecules or embedded fluorescent substances during the preparation process of MIPs.

The fluorescence enhancement or quenching phenomenon of fluorescent MIPs is generated in the presence of template molecules, and which can be further utilized for sensing template molecules (Tang et al., 2015; Ton et al., 2015; Hou et al., 2016; Wagner et al., 2018). Among the most fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have gained extensive applications in the determination of various pollutants owing to their advantages of low toxicity, ease of manufacture, outstanding aqueous solubility and superior fluorescence stability (Li et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2018; Tao et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018b; Zheng et al., 2018; Zhi et al., 2018).

Herein, a novel fluorescent probe was constructed with molecularly imprinted polymers capped carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) using BPA, 4-vinylpyridine, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate and azodiisobutyronitrile as the template, functional monomer, cross linker and initiator, respectively. The morphology, structure and properties of CDs@MIPs were furthermore well characterized. The fluorescence signal of CDs@MIPs would be changed after binding with template molecules. The perfect combination of the specificity of MIPs and the high sensitivity of fluorescence detection remarkably improved the selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability of this fluorescent probe. What's more, some critical experimental parameters containing the concentration of CDs@MIPs, pH value and the incubation time had been optimized to gain better detection results. The preparation of the fluorescent probe and the principle for the detection of BPA were displayed in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence quenching might be as a result of the electron transfer between amino-functionalized CDs and BPA. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs@MIPs turned out to be proportional to the concentration of BPA. Moreover, the proposed fluorescent probe provides a new method for reliable measurement of BPA and opens up a new avenue for the application in the fluorescent probe of CDs@MIPs.

BPA (80-05-7, >99%), 4-vinylpyridine (4-VP, 100-43-6, 96%), Citric acid (77-92-9, ≥99.5%), azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 78-67-1, 99%), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 97-90-5, 98%), o-nitrophenol (2-NP, 88-75-5, 98%), tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA, 79-94-7, 98%) and quinine sulfate (6119-70-6, 99%) were supplied by Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phenol (PhOH, 108-95-2, ≥99%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company. Ethylenediamine (107-15-3, ≥99%) and hydroquinone (HQ, 123-31-9, 99%) were obtained from Guangfu Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Methanol (HPLC grade, 67-56-1, ≥99.9%) was obtained from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). Ethanol (64-17-5, 99.7%) and other chemicals were of analytical grade. Pure water was utilized all over the experimental procedure.

Citric acid (1.921 g) was mixed with 20 mL pure water and 1.1 mL ethylenediamine was further added into the solution drop by drop. After vigorous stirring, the clear solution was transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle and heated at 240 °C for 3 h. After the temperature of kettle decreased to ambient temperature, petroleum ether was firstly added into the solution and the solution was separated by centrifuging at 11,000 rpm for 10 min. Then ethanol was added and the solution was centrifuged to remove residual reagents. Finally, the precipitate was redissolved into 2 mL of water and dried in an oven to gain the CDs.

To a 150 mL conical flask, CDs (20 mg), BPA (300 mg) and 4-VP (400 μL) were dissolved into 100 mL ethanol and the mixture was dispersed completely by stirring for 3 h at ambient temperature in the dark. Then the cross-linker EGDMA (400 μL) and the initiator AIBN (50 mg) were added. The mixture was blown with nitrogen stream for half an hour and further heated at 60 °C in a water bath with the agitation speed of 700 rpm for one day. The achieved CDs@MIPs were separated by centrifuging and eluted to get rid of the template molecules with ethanol, which was carried out for several times until no template molecules were detected in the eluent on a UV–vis spectrophotometry. The CDs@MIPs powders were gained after drying under vacuum. Meanwhile, non-imprinted polymers capped CDs (CDs@NIPs) were also prepared following the similar steps except that no template molecules were added.

The morphology and size investigations are achieved using a JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Akishima-shi, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) determinations are obtained using a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrophotometer (Thermo, New York,NY, USA). Fluorescent analysis is performed on an F-7000 fluorescent spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Ultraviolet visible absorption spectra are performed on a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SDPTOP, Shanghai, China).
Fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs is calculated based on the method reported (Borse et al., 2017).

100 μL of CDs@MIPs solution (250 μg mL−1) and various concentration of BPA were mixed completely in a centrifuge tube, and further diluted to 1 mL with pure water. The fluorescent responses were gathered with excitation at 353 nm and constant slit widths of 5 nm. Other phenolic compounds such as TBBPA, 2-NP, PhOH and HQ were employed to evaluate the disturbance of concomitant phenolic compounds and meanwhile estimate the selectivity for determining BPA. Furthermore, the control analysis with CDs@NIPs (non-imprinted polymers) were carried out through the same procedure like CDs@MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of BPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability and reliability, three real water samples and plastic cup were used to validate the fluorescent probe for sensing BPA. The real water samples were collected from Changping Park, Chaobaihe River and the Ming Tombs Reservoir (Beijing, China), respectively. The plastic cup was purchased from the local supermarket (Beijing, China). For the real water samples, 50 mL aqueous sample was extracted with 10 mL CHCl3 with a separatory funnel for three times, and the organic phase was blown to dryness with nitrogen stream. The extracted substances were redissolved with methanol (2.0 mL) by shaking carefully, which was ready for analysis. For the plastic cup sample, 200 mg accurately weighed plastic cup sample was extracted using 10 mL CHCl3 through ultrasound for 30 min and separated by centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The supernate was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and blown to near dryness with nitrogen stream. The sample were detected after redissolving with 2.0 mL methanol. As no obvious BPA in the real samples was detected, the spiked samples were further analyzed.

The prepared CDs were characterized and illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The CDs were uniform with nearly spherical structures and had a mean diameter of 6.3 nm according to the analytical results from 105 random nanoparticles. The surface feature groups of the CDs were further identified by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2B). The typical peak at about 3270 cm−1 was accounted for the stretching-vibration of O–H/N–H. The peak centered at 2930 cm−1 indicates the existence of C–H, while the stretching-vibration of C=O was located at 1660 cm−1. The bands at 1556, 1384 and 1049 cm−1 were possibly put down to the N–H/C=C bending-vibration, C–N and C–O stretching-vibration, respectively (Hou et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2017). These strong hydrophilic groups on the prepared CDs not only strengthened their solubility in water greatly, but also facilitated the formation of massive defects on the surface of CDs and further served as a serious of the emissive traps which emitted light effectually upon excitation (Hassan et al., 2018). In order to investigate the optical properties of the CDs, more detail researches were carried out by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2C, two obvious absorption peaks were located at 238 and 347 nm, respectively. The UV absorption band at 238 nm was assigned to π-π* transition of C=C bond and the other band at 347 nm is derived from n-π* transition that offered the emissive traps and excitation surface states, which can further lead to the fluorescent enhancement (Yuan et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016). The excitation-independent feature of the CDs was clearly displayed in Fig. 2D. As in the case of most CDs, the corresponding fluorescence emission peak still maintained at 439 nm, no noticeable blue shifting or red shifting occurred following the increase of the excitation changed from 290 nm to 400 nm. This phenomenon might be resulted from more uniform size of the CDs instead of the surface states, which was in accordance with the TEM result (Chandra et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2018). The QY of the CDs was as high as 62.8%, obtained by using quinine sulfate as the reference (Borse et al., 2017), and the QY was remarkably near to most of the CDs reported in the literatures (Mao et al., 2018; Meng et al., 2018; Shahamirifard et al., 2018). The influences of the sample pH value and salt concentration (based on addition of NaCl) on the fluorescent stability of the CDs were further researched. As seen from Fig. 2E, the fluorescent intensity signal of the CDs exhibited a steep increase as pH from 1 to 4, and achieved the highest value at pH 8 and then decreased obviously from pH 11 to 14. The fluorescent intensity signal kept almost constant over pH 5–11. As given from Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity signal exhibited no visible change with the increase of the concentration of NaCl even which was up to 3.0 M, suggesting the prepared CDs owned strong photostability and resistance to high ionic strength. Hence, these experimental results indicated that the prepared CDs had a promising application potential in physiological field (Liu et al., 2016a).

Fig. 3 A depicts that the CDs@MIPs had both porous cross-linked mesh and interwoven near-spherical morphology. The CDs@MIPs had no obvious absorption peak ranged from 200 nm to 560 nm, which also exhibited that template molecules in the CDs@MIPs had been successfully removed. Additionally, the CDs@MIPs had a fluorescent emission peak at 439 nm under the excitation at 353 nm (Fig. 3B).

In order to further ascertain the successful imprinting and removal of template molecule BPA in polymers, FTIR spectra of CDs@NIPs (non-imprinted polymers) and CDs@MIPs were performed when they were before and after removal of template molecules. As displayed in curve 1 of Fig. 3C, the CDs@MIPs had a typical peak at 1710 cm−1 before removing the template molecule, and the CDs@NIPs had a typical peak in 1732 cm−1 (curve 3 of Fig. 3C). This phenomenon of approximate 22 cm−1 displacement was put down to the effect of hydrogen bond force on molecular imprinted materials, that is to say, the existence of hydrogen bond force in the materials made the characteristic peak shift to the lower wavenumber (Liu et al., 2016b). After removing the template molecules, the characteristic peak was changed from 1710 cm−1 to 1735 cm−1 (curve 2 of Fig. 3C). These experimental results indicated that the template molecule had been firstly imprinted successfully in the polymers and then washed out effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for BPA detection based on CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, the CDs@NIPs displayed much strong fluorescent signal (curve 1 of Fig. 4A) whereas CDs@MIPs with template molecule BPA exhibited weak fluorescence signal (curve 2 of Fig. 4A). When the template molecule BPA was removed, the fluorescent intensity of CDs@MIPs was greatly restored (curve 3 of Fig. 4A), but the fluorescent signal of CDs@MIPs without the template molecule BPA was distinctly quenched after the addition of BPA (curve 4 of Fig. 4A). The quenching of fluorescence might be due to the electron transfer between CDs and BPA. In the detection system, the CDs and BPA were electron donor and electron acceptor, respectively. The electron transfer was more favorable to the occurrence by the narrowing of the distance between CDs and BPA via the formation of hydrogen bonds between the amino-functionalized CDs and BPA (Ensafi et al., 2017). Hence, the fluorescent response signal of CDs@MIPs was obviously declined after the interaction with BPA. For the sake of achieving better detection sensitivity, the concentration of CDs@MIPs, pH value and reaction time were optimized and their influences on the quenching efficiency (F0/F1-1) were further investigated under different conditions (F0 and F1 were the fluorescence intensity of the probe without and with BPA).

Fig. 4B demonstrates that the quenching efficiency was remarkably affected by the concentration of CDs@MIPs. The quenching efficiency was firstly increased and then decreased along with the raise of the probe concentration. When the probe concentration was 25 μg mL−1, the quenching efficiency reached the highest. The quenching effect was influenced by the insufficient interaction between BPA and the probe at the low probe concentration, while the too high probe concentration caused the fluorescence intensity of the probe too strong which further made the quenching efficiency decrease (Liu et al., 2021). In other words, a higher sensitivity and a wider detection range would be obtained under the appropriate concentration of CDs@MIPs. In short, 25 μg mL−1 was selected as the best probe concentration.

As displayed in Fig. 4C, the pH value was very important in the analytical system. The quenching efficiency was also first increased and then decreased with the increase of the pH value. The best quenching effect was gained at pH 7. The results suggested that the quenching efficiency would decrease under acidic or alkaline conditions, and the interaction force between the probe and BPA would be affected by the acidic or alkaline environment. Therefore, the neutral condition (pH = 7) was used for further experiments.

Fig. 4D reveals the effect of the reaction time on fluorescence quenching performance. The results showed that quenching efficiency was gradually increased and then was stabilized with the prolongation of reaction time. The quenching efficiency kept stable after 2 min, which indicated the fluorescent probe completely interacted with BPA. In order to ensure the accuracy and efficiency, the optimal reaction time was determined to be 3 min.

The fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent probe were measured for quantification of BPA under the optimum detection conditions. Fig. S1A shows that the fluorescent responses of the probe gradually decreased with increasing concentration of BPA. Compared with CDs@MIPs, the fluorescent responses of CDs@NIPs slightly decreased with spiking the same concentration of BPA. Quenching efficiency was much lower than that of CDs@MIPs (Fig. S1B), which indicated that the imprinting not only improved the selectivity but also enhanced the sensitivity of the probe. As displayed in Fig. S1C, the slope of the two linear relationships was rather different, revealing that the sensitivity of CDs@MIPs for BPA was better than that of CDs@NIPs. It was attributed to the fact that the molecularly imprinted materials had the better spatial matching ability for BPA. There was an excellent linearity between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of this fluorescent probe and BPA concentration over range of 10–2000 nM (r2 = 0.9999). The detection limit of this method was as low as 1.5 nM (3σ/S, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signals, and S was the slope of the linear calibration curve). In comparison with some reported probes, this proposed probe possessed a comparable linear range and superior detection limit (Table 1 ).

For testing the selectivity of the developed fluorescent probe, the effects of various potential phenolic compounds including tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA), phenol (PhOH), o-nitrophenol (2-NP) and hydroquinone (HQ) were considered. As shown in Fig. 5 A, compared with obvious fluorescence quenching by BPA, the quenching efficiency of CD@MIPs was not changed by the introduction of other phenolic compounds, suggesting that this probe had ideal selectivity for the determination of BPA. What's more, other coexistent substances exhibited no obviously interference when both BPA and other phenolic compounds were in the detection system. The selectivity of CDs@NIPs was also performed (Fig. 5B) and the data revealed that the detection of BPA by CDs@NIPs was seriously affected by the presence of other interference compounds. All these further demonstrated that the prepared CDs@MIPs had a unique recognition performance for BPA and the designed probe is highly selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproductivity and stability for determination of BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The reliability and stability of this designed fluorescent probe were further accessed by carrying out series of experiments to obtain intra-day, inter-day precisions and stability. For obtaining the intra-day precision, the fluorescent intensity signals of this probe were recorded with BPA concentration of 25 nM, 100 nM and 250 nM six times a day, while in the inter-day precision experiment, the above-mentioned three levels were measured once a day and totally carried out in six consecutive days. The obtained data demonstrated that the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of intra-day precision of 25 nM, 100 nM and 250 nM were 4.4, 3.3 and 1.3%, respectively, and the RSDs of inter-day precision were 4.7, 2.7 and 0.63%, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescent intensity signal of the probe to BPA spiked at 100 nM within 24 h was measured. As shown in Fig. S2, the quenching efficiency was kept unchangeable within 6 h and then was slightly decreased after 8 h, but the change was rather small, and then there was no significant change until 24 h. The experimental results forcefully indicated that this developed method possessed the good reliability and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To access the possibility of this probe, it was utilized to detect BPA in three kinds of real water samples and one kind of plastic cup. As shown in Table 2 , the spiked recoveries ranged from 97.2% to 103.4% and the RSDs of six repeated detections for each sample were below 4.5%, which indicated that the novel constructed fluorescent probe was a promising means for BPA determination in various samples.

This study designed a new fluorescent probe with molecularly imprinted polymers capped carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) and which were successfully synthesized. This new fluorescent probe was highly sensitive to BPA. Under optimum conditions, the probe had an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of BPA of 10–2000 nM, and the detection limit was low down to 1.5 nM. The data above exhibited that the developed fluorescent probe owned an outstanding imprinting effect and selectivity for BPA and had been successfully utilized to monitor BPA in real samples. The proposed fluorescent probe had a potential application prospect in the environmental field.

Yongli Liu: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yue Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jingwen Niu: Investigation, Software. Linchun Nie: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shiyu Huang: Formal analysis. Huanhuan Liu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Investigation, Software, Validation. Investigation, Software, Validation. Shuai Yuan: Investigation, Software, Validation. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2023.138303.
